subject: PAR-25-450: Clinical Trial Readiness for Rare Diseases, Disorders, and Syndromes (R21 Exploratory/Developmental Research Grant - Clinical Trial Not Allowed)

content.title: Evidence Supporting Rare Disease Classification
subtitles: Must provide evidence disease affects ≤200,000 individuals in the United States
Include references confirming disease/condition prevalence
If disease granted orphan status by FDA, provide this information
If rare variant/subset of common condition: provide rationale for focusing trial readiness study on this variant
Describe scientific basis for separating biomarker/COA validation for rare variant from common condition
Section required with heading: 'Evidence Supporting Rare Disease Classification'
Application considered non-responsive and will not be reviewed if missing this section

content.title: Urgent Need for Clinical Trial Readiness
subtitles: Describe need for conducting trial readiness study at this time
Explain how project addresses critical barrier or bottleneck to progress toward clinical trials
Describe clinical trial design issues to be addressed: biomarker/COA validation, power calculations, inclusion/exclusion criteria, trial duration
Describe potential impact of proposed studies on design and increasing likelihood of success of upcoming clinical trials
Provide brief description of candidate therapeutics/devices development state (even if applicant not involved)
Provide timelines for advance of therapeutics/devices to clinical trials
Describe clinical trial(s) enabled by results of this trial readiness study
Provide letters of support from researchers expecting to conduct upcoming trials
Outline next steps for moving into clinical trial if proposed study successful
Provide rationale that clinical trial is feasible in near future
Describe currently available COA measures and/or biomarkers
If appropriate, describe how study will result in advancements over currently available measures/biomarkers
If current measures/biomarkers inadequate or insufficiently developed: describe limitations and how they may compromise trial success
If trial readiness study is ancillary to ongoing clinical trial or longitudinal study: describe that study and provide letter of support from lead PD(s)/PI(s)
Explain how trial readiness study will lead to improvements in future trial design above ongoing study
Describe gaps in disease natural history knowledge that need addressing to achieve clinical trial readiness
Explain how lack of this knowledge will compromise clinical trial design and how study overcomes obstacles
Describe role of companies or voluntary health organizations engaged or potentially available for clinical trial readiness studies or clinical trials
Section required with heading: 'Urgent Need for Clinical Trial Readiness'

content.title: Biomarkers and Context of Use
subtitles: Use BEST Resource terminology from FDA-NIH Biomarker Working Group (https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK338448)
Biomarker definition: defined characteristic measured as indicator of normal biological processes, pathogenic processes, or responses to intervention
Biomarker types: molecular, histologic, radiographic, physiologic
Biomarker categories: susceptibility/risk, diagnostic, monitoring, prognostic, predictive, pharmacodynamic/response, safety
Clinical Outcome Assessment (COA): assessment of how individual feels, functions, or survives
COA types: clinician-reported, observer-reported, patient-reported, performance outcomes
Context of Use (COU): statement fully describing how medical product development tool is used and the purpose
Concept: aspect of individual's clinical, biological, physical, or functional state that assessment captures
Validation: process establishing test/tool/instrument performance acceptable for intended purpose
Clinical validation: establishing that test/tool acceptably identifies, measures, or predicts concept of interest
Analytical validation: establishing technical performance (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, precision) with specified protocol
Biochemical and molecular biomarkers should have substantial analytical validation data prior to application submission

content.title: Specific Aims
subtitles: Concise statement of research goals and objectives
Clear articulation of hypotheses to be tested
Explanation of expected outcomes and significance
Typically 1 page for R21 applications

content.title: Research Strategy: Significance
subtitles: Explain importance of problem or critical barrier to progress in field
Describe how proposed project will improve scientific knowledge, technical capability, clinical practice
Explain how project addresses significant needs in design and increasing likelihood of success of upcoming clinical trials
Describe how concepts, methods, technologies, treatments, interventions, or preventive strategies that drive field will be changed if aims achieved

content.title: Research Strategy: Innovation
subtitles: Explain how application challenges and seeks to shift current research or clinical practice paradigms
Describe novel theoretical concepts, approaches, methodologies, instrumentation, or interventions
Explain refinements, improvements, or new applications of theoretical concepts, approaches or methodologies, instrumentation or interventions
For clinical trial readiness: innovation in biomarker/COA development or natural history characterization approaches

content.title: Research Strategy: Approach
subtitles: Describe overall strategy, methodology, and analyses including means for data collection and analysis
Describe preliminary studies, data, and/or experience relevant to application
If using existing resources: describe how they will be leveraged (RDCRN, NeuroNEXT, other rare disease networks)
If leveraging resources from advocacy groups, private foundations, academic institutions, other government agencies, NIH Intramural program: describe
Describe research design and methods for achieving specific aims
Explain expected results, potential problems, alternative strategies
For biomarker/outcome measure studies: describe validation approach and context of use
For natural history studies: describe data collection methods, participant characterization, analysis plan
Statistical plan: describe sample size calculations, power analysis, statistical methods
Timeline with milestones for project completion
Plan for dissemination of findings to enable upcoming clinical trials

content.title: Bibliography & References Cited
subtitles: List all references cited in Research Strategy
Follow NIH citation format guidelines
Include author names, article title, journal, volume, pages, year
References not counted in page limits for Research Strategy

content.title: Senior/Key Person Profile: Biosketches
subtitles: NIH Biographical Sketch format required for all senior/key personnel
Include: Personal Statement, Positions and Honors, Contributions to Science, Research Support
Biosketches limited to 5 pages per person
List publications most relevant to application
Describe role in proposed research

content.title: Senior/Key Person Profile: Current and Pending Support
subtitles: List all current and pending research support for each senior/key person
Include: funding source, project title, direct costs per year, overlap with proposed project
Describe overlap and explain how proposed project is distinct
Updated format following NIH guidelines

content.title: Budget and Budget Justification
subtitles: R21 mechanism: up to $275,000 in direct costs over 2 years (typically $200,000 max total costs)
Follow NIH R21 budget guidelines
Detailed Budget: Personnel, Equipment, Travel, Participant/Trainee Support Costs, Other Direct Costs
Budget Justification: detailed explanation for each budget category
For multi-site studies: include subaward budgets
Justify personnel effort levels
Justify equipment purchases if needed
Describe participant costs if applicable
No preliminary data required for R21, but budget should reflect exploratory nature

content.title: Facilities and Other Resources
subtitles: Describe institutional facilities available for conduct of proposed research
Include: Laboratory, Clinical, Animal, Computer, Office, Other facilities
Describe major equipment available
Explain adequacy of facilities and resources for proposed work
For multi-site studies: describe facilities at each site
If leveraging existing networks/resources: describe access and capabilities

content.title: Equipment
subtitles: List major items of equipment already available for proposed project
Equipment valued at $5,000 or more
Describe how existing equipment supports proposed work
If equipment purchase requested in budget: justify need and explain why existing equipment insufficient

content.title: Resource Sharing Plans
subtitles: Data Sharing Plan: describe what data will be shared, when, how, with whom
Follow NIH Data Sharing Policy requirements
Model Organism Sharing Plan if applicable
Genomic Data Sharing Plan if applicable
Explain any restrictions on data sharing
Describe repository where data will be deposited
Timeline for data sharing relative to publication

content.title: Letters of Support
subtitles: Letters from researchers planning upcoming clinical trials that would be enabled by this work
Letters from collaborating institutions or networks (if applicable)
Letters from patient advocacy organizations (if applicable)
Letters confirming access to patient populations or resources
If ancillary to ongoing study: letter from lead PD(s)/PI(s) of that study
Letters should be specific about commitment and role in proposed project

categories.name: Clinical Trial Readiness Focus
examples: Validation of sensitive, reliable, responsive tools (COA measures, biomarkers)
Tools to identify/select appropriate participants for clinical trials
Tools to measure effects of interventions
Define disease presentation and course essential for clinical trial design
Retrospective, longitudinal, or cross-sectional natural history approaches
Address critical barriers/bottlenecks to clinical trial progress
Power calculations for upcoming trials
Inclusion/exclusion criteria determination
Trial duration determination
Biomarker/COA validation for specific rare disease populations
Candidate therapeutics ready for testing after study completion
Near-term feasibility of moving to clinical trial

categories.name: Rare Disease Requirements
examples: Disease affects ≤200,000 individuals in U.S. (Rare Diseases Act of 2002)
Evidence Supporting Rare Disease Classification section required (non-responsive if missing)
Prevalence references required
FDA orphan status information if applicable
If rare variant of common condition: scientific basis for separate validation required

categories.name: BEST Resource Terminology
examples: Biomarker: defined characteristic indicating biological/pathogenic processes or intervention responses
Clinical Outcome Assessment (COA): how individual feels, functions, survives
Context of Use (COU): statement describing tool use and development purpose
Concept: aspect of state that assessment captures
Clinical validation: test/tool identifies, measures, predicts concept of interest
Analytical validation: technical performance (sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, precision)
COA types: clinician-reported, observer-reported, patient-reported, performance outcomes
Biomarker categories: susceptibility/risk, diagnostic, monitoring, prognostic, predictive, pharmacodynamic, safety
Validation fit-for-purpose within defined context of use
Biochemical/molecular biomarkers need substantial analytical validation prior to submission
BEST Resource glossary: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK338448

categories.name: Leveraging Existing Resources
examples: Rare Diseases Clinical Research Network (RDCRN)
NeuroNEXT network
Other existing rare disease clinical research networks
SMART IRB for multi-center studies
Advocacy groups and private research foundations
Academic institutions and NIH Intramural program
Ancillary studies to ongoing clinical trials or longitudinal studies

categories.name: Research Strategy Components
examples: Specific Aims: goals, objectives, hypotheses, expected outcomes
Significance: importance of problem, impact on field, addressing critical needs
Innovation: novel concepts, approaches, methodologies; paradigm shifts
Approach: overall strategy, methodology, preliminary data, research design, expected results, statistical plan, timeline

categories.name: Application Format
examples: SF424(R&R) Application Components required
PHS 398 Research Plan required
NIH Biographical Sketch format (5 pages per person)
Current and Pending Support documentation
Budget following R21 guidelines
Facilities and Other Resources description
Resource Sharing Plans (Data, Model Organism, Genomic if applicable)
Letters of Support from upcoming trial researchers

constraints.value: Must follow NIH SF424(R&R) Application Guide Table of Page Limits
Evidence Supporting Rare Disease Classification section required (non-responsive if missing)
Urgent Need for Clinical Trial Readiness section required
Must use BEST Resource terminology for biomarkers/COAs
Clinical trials NOT allowed (R21 Clinical Trial Not Allowed mechanism)
Candidate therapeutics must be ready for testing after study completion
constraints.section: Urgent Need for Clinical Trial Readiness

constraints.value: Biochemical/molecular biomarkers need substantial analytical validation data before submission
Letters of support required from researchers conducting upcoming enabled trials
constraints.section: Urgent Need for Clinical Trial Readiness

constraints.value: Disease must affect ≤200,000 people in U.S.
Equipment list: major items $5,000 or more
